## How does Hybe define its purpose and direction?

Mission and vision anchor Hybe’s strategic focus, guiding investments in IP, platforms, and global fandom commerce. Clear statements speed execution and shape investor confidence amid rapid industry shifts. Hybe leverages labels, Weverse, concerts, and IP businesses to scale globally.

![What are Mission Vision & Core Values of Hybe Company?](//portersfiveforce.com/cdn/shop/articles/hybecorp-mission_800x.png?v=1762985288)

Mission, vision, and core values direct where Hybe allocates capital, builds fandom ecosystems, and balances artist development with platform growth. They inform bets on IP, tech, and M&A to convert attention into ARPU and sustain global expansion.

What are Mission Vision & Core Values of Hybe Company? Find strategic context and frameworks like Hybe Porter's Five Forces Analysis

## Key Takeaways

## Mission: What is Hybe Mission Statement?

Companys’s mission is 'to amplify artists and connect global fans through music-driven platforms that create lasting cultural and commercial value.'

Hybe mission centers on empowering artists and engaging global fans via music production, artist management, platform services (Weverse), live experiences, and IP expansion across media and commerce.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Official Mission

Officially summarized as 'We believe in music,' emphasizing music-first purpose for fans and artists.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Target Audiences

Targets global fans and artists across genres, markets, and age groups with localized content and global distribution.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Core Offerings

Includes music creation, multi-label A&R, artist management, live events, merchandising, and digital fandom services.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Platform Strategy

Weverse integrates content, commerce and community; by 2024 it hosted 100+ artists and improved D2C take rates versus third-party marketplaces.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Multi-Label Structure

Brands like BigHit Music, PLEDIS, BELIFT LAB, Source Music, KOZ and Ithaca Holdings allow diversified A&R while centralizing platform ops.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Value Creation

Focuses on maximizing artist IP lifetime value through integrated content, merchandising, gaming, webtoons and education extensions.

Hybe vision emphasizes global leadership in entertainment via platformization, data-driven monetization, and expanding fandom economies across media and commerce.

Mission: 'We believe in music.' Analysis: targets global fans & artists; offers music, management, live experiences, Weverse platform and IP extensions; scope is global multi-label; unique value is platform-integrated IP monetization. See [Owners & Shareholders of Hybe](/blogs/mission/hybecorp)

## Hybe SWOT Analysis

![Get Related Template](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0914/5276/8633/files/hybecorp-swot-analysis.png)

## Vision: What is Hybe Vision Statement?

Companys’s vision is 'to innovate the music industry through technology and a platform that connects and expands the artist–fan experience.'

Hybe's vision focuses on building a global fandom OS that combines artist development, content, live experiences and commerce to scale fandom engagement and IP value across industries while expanding localized presence in key markets.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Scope

End-to-end IP ownership from discovery to touring and commerce, aiming to shift label economics to platform multiples via Weverse and cross-vertical licensing.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Realism

With **KRW 2.17T** revenue and **KRW 295B+** operating income in 2023 and multiple million-seller releases in 2024, the Hybe vision is financially credible.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Aspiration

Expand beyond K-pop into education, gaming and immersive experiences to raise non-album revenue share and global market penetration.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Strategic Focus

Scale Weverse as the central fandom platform and pursue IP licensing, joint ventures and localized A&R in North America and Japan.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Metrics

Target higher non-album revenue mix, increased ARPU on fandom platform, and diversified IP royalties across media and education verticals.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Stakeholder Impact

Enhance artist careers and fan monetization while aligning Hybe mission, Hybe vision and Hybe core values to sustainable growth and social responsibility; see [Brief History of Hybe](/blogs/owners/hybecorp) for context.

Official vision commonly referenced: 'To innovate the music industry through technology and a platform that connects and expands the artist–fan experience.' This underpins Hybe mission, Hybe company purpose and Hybe corporate philosophy focused on fandom-driven growth.

## Hybe PESTLE Analysis

![Get Related Template](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0914/5276/8633/files/hybecorp-pestle-analysis.png)

## Values: What is Hybe Core Values Statement?

Hybe core values center on artist-first development, platform-driven innovation, global collaboration, and fan-centric community trust; these guide product pacing, data-led operations, and sustainability efforts across labels and services. The values shape Hybe mission, Hybe vision and corporate philosophy toward scalable, artist-respecting global entertainment.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Hybe prioritizes long-term artist investment, multi-year training, creative control frameworks and mental-health support, emphasizing quality-over-quantity releases and fair crediting/profit-sharing to protect artist careers.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Platform thinking powers unified fan IDs, Weverse integrations, real‑time fan-data for touring/merch decisions and pilots in digital collectibles and in-app ticketing to reduce bots and boost lifetime value.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Hybe executes cross-border A&R and strategic partnerships (Universal, global promoters), onboarding non-Hybe artists to Weverse to expand reach and diversify revenue across Korea, US and Japan markets.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Safe community guidelines, moderation, exclusive membership benefits and transparent crisis communications build fan loyalty, reducing churn and supporting higher ARPPU and engagement metrics.

Read how Hybe mission and Hybe vision influence strategic decisions, resource allocation and artist partnerships next: [Mission, Vision & Core Values of Hybe](/blogs/brief-history/hybecorp)

Values — Artist-first development and integrity: multi-year training, creative control and mental-health support for long-cycle bets like BTS and SEVENTEEN; Innovation and platform thinking: Weverse, unified IDs, real-time fan data and anti-bot ticketing; Global openness: multi-label cross-border A&R and UMG partnerships; Fan-centric community: moderation, exclusive content and transparent communication; Social responsibility: UNICEF donations, greener touring pilots; Operational excellence: KPI-driven metrics (DAU/MAU, ARPPU, sell-through) and centralized supply chain to capture margin and improve delivery.

## Hybe Business Model Canvas

![Get Related Template](https://cdn.shopify.com/s/files/1/0914/5276/8633/files/hybecorp-business-model-canvas.png)

## How Mission & Vision Influence Hybe Business?

Mission and vision statements shape strategic allocation, product roadmaps, and talent priorities; they guide HYBE's shift from an artist-centric label to a platform-driven global entertainment group. Clear purpose and values influence M&A, platform investments, and operational KPIs across music, merchandising, touring, and tech.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Star-Icon.svg)

### HYBE's Mission, Vision & Core Values

Concise framing of what drives HYBE's decisions: artist-first music creation amplified by technology and global business models.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Product & Platform Alignment

Mission drove Weverse expansion beyond HYBE acts; by 2024 Weverse hosted top-tier non-HYBE acts, producing double-digit YoY commerce growth and rising paid memberships.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Market Expansion

Vision guided US and Japan scale-up via HYBE America and local A&R; Japan historically contributes around **30%** of K-pop export value and remained a key revenue source in 2023–2024.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### M&A & Partnerships

Acquisitions such as Ithaca Holdings (2021) and subsequent label additions illustrate a platform-plus-IP thesis, supported by strategic partnerships and touring resumption after 2022.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Touring & Merch

2024 tours from major HYBE acts delivered record merch per-cap in select venues, leveraging fan data for pricing and inventory decisions.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Operations & Data Discipline

Values around data drove inventory optimization and D2C margin expansion; in-app ticketing pilots in 2024 materially reduced fraud in targeted markets.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Leadership Messaging

Executives emphasize 'music at the center, technology as the amplifier,' channeling capital to Weverse, global A&R, and training initiatives.

Mission and vision directly influence strategic choices—product expansion, M&A, market focus, and ops discipline—read the next chapter on Core Improvements to Company's Mission and Vision to see recommended shifts and metrics.

Influence Strategy alignment:

Leadership signaling: HYBE leadership repeatedly emphasizes ‘music at the center, technology as the amplifier,’ aligning capital to Weverse, training, and global A&R.

## What Are Mission & Vision Improvements?

Four focused improvements can make Hybe mission, vision and core values more measurable, inclusive and aligned with 2025 industry expectations. These enhancements translate strategy into investor-grade KPIs and operational commitments to artists, fans and global markets.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Codify target outcomes such as serving **100M** monthly fandom users and achieving **50%**+ of revenue from platform/IP services by 2027 to convert Hybe mission into investor-grade KPIs and clearer Hybe vision execution metrics.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Explicitly state expansion beyond K-pop into multi-genre global IP with priority market targets in the US, Latin America and Southeast Asia to reduce key-artist concentration risk and clarify Hybe company purpose.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Integrate science-based targets for tour emissions and sustainable merchandise plus accessibility goals aligned with major label net-zero roadmaps to reflect Hybe core values and corporate philosophy in 2025 reporting.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Commit to generative AI policy, short-form discovery optimization, immersive concert investment and standardized artist welfare across labels (including third-party onboarding to Weverse) to operationalize Hybe cultural values.

Improvements

For context on Hybe strategic vision and long-term goals and how these changes map to business model choices, see [Growth Strategy of Hybe](/blogs/growth-strategy/hybecorp).

## How Does Hybe Implement Corporate Strategy?

Implementation of mission and vision in corporate strategy requires operationalizing goals into measurable objectives and embedding them into daily workflows to drive consistent outcomes across the organization.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Star-Icon.svg)

### Hybe mission, vision & core values — at a glance

Clear purpose statements guide product, artist and commerce decisions across the group.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

### Strategic metrics

OKRs align to MAU, conversion, take rate and NPS; platform KPIs drive monetization and retention.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

### Artist ecosystem

Multi-label greenlight committees and stage-gate funding with dashboards for trainee progression and projected LTV.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

### Commerce & fulfillment

Centralized D2C via Weverse Shop improves gross margins; pre-order signals and geo heatmaps optimize tour routing.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

### Governance & culture

Platform Council aligns labels, legal and data science; quarterly town halls and leadership roadshows cascade mission and vision.

Implementation

Platform execution: Weverse product sprints on identity, moderation, and in-app ticketing; OKRs tied to **MAU**, conversion, take rate, and NPS. Formal governance: cross-functional Platform Council aligning labels, legal, and data science to weekly priorities.

Artist development system: Multi-label greenlight committees with stage-gate funding; dashboards track trainee progression, content readiness, and projected LTV. Values reinforced via training, creator credits, and wellness programs.

Live and merch operations: Centralized D2C through Weverse Shop for predictable fulfillment and higher gross margins; dynamic demand planning uses pre-order signals and geo heatmaps for tour routing and drops.

Communication: Quarterly town halls cascade mission/vision; leadership roadshows with investors and partners; creator guidelines embedded in contracts and onboarding. External alignment via CSR reporting on donations, education, and environmental pilots.

Programs/systems: Ethics and compliance training for staff; community standards and reporting tools in-app; incident response protocols to protect artist and fan safety; KPI-linked compensation includes platform growth and artist satisfaction indices.

Financial & scale facts: As of 2024 HYBE reported consolidated revenue of **KRW 2.1 trillion** (approx. USD 1.6 billion) and a global fan-platform MAU growth exceeding **20% year‑over‑year** in major markets; platform commerce contributed a material share of recurring revenue through Weverse Shop and ticketing.

Culture & purpose signals: Hybe mission and Hybe vision drive investments in creator wellbeing, IP diversification, and global partnerships; Hybe core values emphasize artist-first decision making and cross-functional collaboration.

For context on audience targeting and market positioning, see [Target Market of Hybe](/blogs/target-market/hybecorp)

# Exploring the World of HYBE Corporation & Entertainment

Hey everyone! Ever wondered about the inner workings of the entertainment industry, especially when it comes to the juggernauts like HYBE Corporation? You know, the folks behind BTS and a whole slew of other amazing artists? Well, buckle up, because we’re about to dive deep into the world of HYBE, exploring everything from their digital strategies to the global impact they’ve made. Let’s get started, shall we?

## Decoding “psewww bighit entertainment comse” – What’s the Deal?

So, you might be wondering, what’s with the “psewww bighit entertainment comse” thing? Honestly, it seems like a bit of a typo or a scrambled URL related to the old Big Hit Entertainment website. Before HYBE Corporation was established, Big Hit Entertainment was the company behind the phenomenal success of BTS. The “comse” could be a potential abbreviation for “commercial site” or perhaps a typo of “company site”. It’s crucial to understand that HYBE has since evolved beyond just music; it’s a multi-faceted entertainment and lifestyle company. However, the legacy of Big Hit is undeniable and continues to influence HYBE’s success. It really just underscores the dynamic evolution of the entertainment industry. The internet can be a bit tricky with these URLs and initial company names, so it’s always good to double-check! This evolution is a testament to their growth, strategic vision, and adaptability to the rapidly changing digital landscape. *HYBE* has successfully broadened its reach beyond music. They have strategically expanded their business to encompass a diverse portfolio, including gaming, content creation, and technology. This diversification helps them in maintaining a competitive edge and appeal to a broader audience. These strategic moves have allowed HYBE to strengthen its position in the entertainment industry and grow. This adaptability and innovation are major reasons why they have become so well-known and successful. HYBE’s commitment to innovation and adaptability, as demonstrated by its evolving online presence and strategic business decisions, is truly impressive. This transformation from a music label to a diversified entertainment powerhouse is a journey many companies can learn from.

### The Rise of HYBE: From Big Hit to Global Phenomenon

Okay, let’s rewind a bit and talk about the origins. Before HYBE, there was Big Hit Entertainment. **Big Hit Entertainment**, under the visionary leadership of Bang Si-hyuk, took a chance on a group of young, ambitious artists called BTS. This move was a game-changer. Bang Si-hyuk’s knack for identifying talent, coupled with a deep understanding of music and the industry, set the stage for something incredible. Big Hit Entertainment focused on a business model that was very different from what was usual back then. They prioritized artists’ creative control and direct engagement with fans. This approach helped the artists, and it also built a dedicated and devoted fan base called the ARMY. The company didn’t just see BTS as a music group. They saw a whole world of possibilities. ## Decoding HYBE Corporation: What Drives Its Success?

Understanding the company's core principles is key to grasping its strategic moves and industry dominance. Delving into the [Hybe SWOT Analysis](/products/hybecorp-swot-analysis) can further illuminate these aspects.

Discover how the company defines success and shapes its corporate culture through its guiding principles, providing a roadmap for its future plans and vision.

## Key Takeaways

## Mission: What is Hybe Mission Statement?

HYBE's mission is 'to create and share experiences that move hearts and inspire hope through music and entertainment.'

Let's delve into the heart of HYBE's operations: its mission. The Hybe mission is not just a statement; it's the guiding principle behind every decision, every artist developed, and every fan interaction. It's about more than just selling music; it's about building a global community connected by shared experiences. Understanding the Hybe mission is crucial for anyone seeking to understand the Hybe company and its impact.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### The Core of Connection

At its core, the Hybe mission revolves around the power of music to connect people. This connection transcends geographical boundaries and cultural differences. It fosters a sense of belonging and shared experience among fans worldwide.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Inspiring Joy and Hope

The mission emphasizes the ability of music and entertainment to inspire joy and hope. HYBE aims to create content that uplifts and provides positive experiences for its audience. This is a key element of the Hybe vision.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Global Audience Focus

HYBE's mission is explicitly targeted at a global audience. This global perspective influences its content creation, artist selection, and marketing strategies. The success of the BTS agency, for example, is a testament to this global reach.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Empowering Artists

A key element of the mission is empowering artists. HYBE provides its artists with the resources and support they need to thrive creatively and commercially. This includes comprehensive artist development programs.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Building a Vibrant Community

HYBE actively fosters a vibrant community around its artists and content. This community-building approach strengthens the bond between artists and fans. Platforms like Weverse are central to this strategy.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Driving Innovation

The mission extends beyond traditional entertainment, driving innovation in areas like technology and content creation. This includes exploring new business models and expanding into diverse entertainment experiences. This is a key element in Hybe's future plans and vision.

The Hybe mission is not just aspirational; it's actively implemented through various initiatives. For example, Weverse, a fan platform, boasts over 100 million users as of late 2024, facilitating direct communication between artists and fans. This platform exemplifies HYBE's commitment to building a strong community. Furthermore, HYBE's expansion into areas like education and gaming demonstrates its dedication to creating diverse entertainment experiences. The company's strategic investments and acquisitions, such as the acquisition of Ithaca Holdings in 2021 (which brought artists like Justin Bieber and Ariana Grande under its umbrella), further illustrate its commitment to its mission. This expansion has significantly increased HYBE's global revenue, with international sales accounting for a substantial portion of its total earnings. You can learn more about this by reading about the [Competitors Landscape of Hybe](/blogs/competitors/hybecorp-competitors).

## Vision: What is Hybe Vision Statement?

HYBE's vision is 'To be the world's top music-based entertainment lifestyle platform company.'

Let's delve into what this ambitious vision entails for the Hybe company.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Global Leadership Ambitions

The core of Hybe's vision is to establish itself as a global leader. This goes beyond simply being a successful music label; it's about dominating the entertainment landscape worldwide. This encompasses not just music but also related lifestyle elements.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Beyond Traditional Music

The vision explicitly targets a "music-based entertainment lifestyle platform." This signals a shift away from the traditional music business model. It includes areas such as gaming, merchandise, and digital content, creating a comprehensive entertainment ecosystem.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Convergence of Music and Technology

Technology is a key component of Hybe's vision and goals. The company is actively integrating technology into its operations, from content creation to distribution. This focus on technology enables new ways for artists and fans to connect.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Innovation and Boundary Pushing

The vision statement implicitly encourages innovation. Hybe aims to push boundaries within the music and entertainment sectors. This involves exploring new business models, technologies, and creative approaches to stay ahead of the curve.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Aspirational Yet Achievable

While ambitious, the vision appears realistic, considering Hybe's strategic moves. The acquisition of Ithaca Holdings in 2021, for example, significantly expanded its global presence and artist roster. The development of Weverse, a platform for fan interaction, further supports the company's goals.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Strategic Initiatives in Action

The company's investments in platform development, content creation, and technology-driven initiatives demonstrate a commitment to achieving its vision. These strategic moves are crucial for realizing its long-term vision for the future. For instance, the Weverse platform had over 10 million monthly active users as of late 2023, showcasing its growing influence.

Understanding the vision is key to grasping Hybe's strategic direction. For a deeper dive into the company's foundational principles, explore the [Mission, Vision & Core Values of Hybe](/blogs/mission/hybecorp-mission).

## Hybe PESTLE Analysis

![Get Related Template](/cdn/shop/files/hybecorp-pestle-analysis.png)

## Values: What is Hybe Core Values Statement?

Understanding the core values of HYBE (formerly Big Hit Entertainment) is crucial to grasping its operational philosophy and its impact on the entertainment industry. These values shape the company's culture, guide its decisions, and influence its interactions with artists, fans, and stakeholders.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Creativity is at the heart of HYBE's operations, driving the diverse artistic output from its multi-label system. This value is evident in the distinct concepts and music styles of groups under different HYBE labels, fostering innovation in content creation, including multimedia content and original stories based on artist IP. For example, the success of groups like BTS, under the HYBE umbrella, showcases this commitment, with their music and concepts constantly evolving to captivate a global audience. This approach has significantly contributed to the company's revenue, with music sales and related content driving a substantial portion of their financial performance.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Collaboration is a key driver for HYBE, evident in its multi-label structure and strategic partnerships. This includes collaborations between artists from different labels, as well as partnerships with global companies for content distribution and market expansion. The company’s collaborative approach to artist management, emphasizing teamwork between the company and the artists, also reflects this value. This collaborative spirit has enabled HYBE to expand its global footprint, forming strategic alliances that boost its reach and influence in the entertainment market.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Inclusivity is a core value, reflected in HYBE's efforts to connect with a global community of fans and provide accessible experiences. This includes offering multi-language support on platforms like Weverse and supporting initiatives that promote positive social change. The company's commitment to inclusivity is evident in its diverse artist roster and its efforts to create content that resonates with a wide range of audiences. The Weverse platform, with its multi-language support, is a prime example of how HYBE fosters a global community, allowing fans worldwide to connect with their favorite artists.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Integrity is a foundational principle that guides decisions and interactions with stakeholders. This involves acting with honesty and transparency in business practices. HYBE's commitment to integrity is critical for maintaining trust with its artists, fans, and investors. This commitment to ethical conduct is essential for long-term sustainability and success in the entertainment industry. This commitment is also reflected in their approach to artist development, ensuring fair practices and fostering a supportive environment.

These core values are central to understanding the Hybe core values and how they shape the company's identity and strategy. They not only define HYBE's approach to artist development and fan engagement but also influence its long-term vision for the future. These Hybe mission and Hybe vision statements are the driving forces behind the company's strategic decisions. To further explore how these values translate into actionable strategies, read the next chapter on how mission and vision influence the company's strategic decisions. For a deeper dive into HYBE's marketing approach and its alignment with these values, consider reading the [Marketing Strategy of Hybe](/blogs/marketing-strategy/hybecorp-marketing-strategy) article.

## How Mission & Vision Influence Hybe Business?

HYBE's unwavering commitment to its mission and vision profoundly shapes its strategic decisions, driving its evolution from a music label to a global entertainment powerhouse. This influence is evident in every facet of its operations, from platform development to market expansion and technological innovation.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Star-Icon.svg)

### Strategic Alignment: Mission & Vision in Action

The core of HYBE's strategy is deeply rooted in its mission and vision. The "We believe in music" mission fuels the company's primary focus, while the vision of becoming a leading global platform company guides its expansion and technological advancements. This alignment ensures that every strategic move contributes to the overarching goals of connecting people through music and entertainment on a global scale.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Weverse's Strategic Role

Weverse's growth is a testament to HYBE's mission and vision. The platform's success in fostering direct artist-fan interaction has significantly contributed to revenue streams. This platform is a key component of HYBE's business model, driving fan engagement and loyalty.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Global Expansion and Market Penetration

HYBE's strategic acquisitions and the "Multi-home, Multi-genre" approach are critical to its global expansion. These initiatives are designed to increase market share and diversify revenue streams. The company's focus remains on connecting with global audiences.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Financial Performance and Diversification

In 2024, HYBE's consolidated revenue reached a record high of ₩2.2545 trillion (approximately $1.6 billion USD), demonstrating the impact of its diversified strategy. While operating profit saw a decrease, the growth in revenue, particularly from concerts and indirect artist involvement, showcases the success of the company's strategic direction. This financial performance reflects the influence of the [Hybe mission](/blogs/brief-history/hybecorp-brief-history).

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Fan Engagement and Revenue Generation

The emphasis on fan engagement through platforms like Weverse has led to substantial revenue from fan clubs and merchandise. This highlights how HYBE's mission and vision directly contribute to its financial success. The company's approach to artist development also plays a crucial role.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Technological Innovation and Future Growth

HYBE's investments in technology and new ventures are a key part of its long-term strategy. These investments are driven by the vision of innovation and adapting to the evolving entertainment landscape. The company is looking to the future.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Lamp-Icon.svg)

### Impact on the Music Industry

HYBE's approach is reshaping the music industry. The company's success provides insights into its corporate culture and how its core values influence decisions. The company's commitment to sustainability is also a factor.

The influence of the Hybe mission, Hybe vision, and Hybe core values is undeniable, shaping every aspect of the company's strategy and driving its remarkable growth. The ability of Hybe company to align its actions with its core principles is a key factor in its success. Next, we will explore the Hybe core values explained and the improvements made to the company's mission and vision.

HYBE actively works to enhance fan experiences and embrace sustainable practices. This includes offering expanded live streaming options for concerts and adopting eco-friendly materials in album production, demonstrating a commitment to both fan satisfaction and environmental responsibility.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

Leadership plays a key role in reinforcing HYBE's mission, vision, and core values. The reorganization into HYBE 2.0, with a focus on Music, Platform, and technological advancements, signals a commitment to the evolving vision and future growth.

![Icon](/cdn/shop/files/BLOG-Content-Resources-Icon.svg)

HYBE communicates its mission, vision, and core values through official statements, reports, and likely integrates them into its corporate culture and external communications. This consistent communication helps to align employees and stakeholders with the company's objectives.

## Music Business Worldwide

# HYBE wants to change fan culture in US, Latin music, and 4 other things we learned on the company’s latest earnings call

K-pop giant HYBE has been expanding internationally in recent years, with notable acquisitions in the US and the Latin music market.

But it seems HYBE’s plan isn’t to just acquire labels and leave them on autopilot. On the company’s latest earnings call, execs hinted at an ambitious agenda: changing the nature of fan culture, presumably to make it more like the carefully produced and highly lucrative world of K-pop fandom. [...] Speaking about the recent launch of KATSEYE – the international girl group that HYBE developed with Universal Music Group imprint Geffen Records – HYBE CEO Jason Jaesang Leesaid the development of KATSEYE is “part of the efforts to revitalize the enthusiastic fandom culture that sort of disappeared in the US after the 1990s.”

That’s certainly an interesting perspective; some would argue that fandom culture didn’t quite disappear, but rather evolved into something new (and decidedly more online).

Yet Lee was likely referring to the popularity of boy and girl groups in the 1990s — think the Spice Girls or the Backstreet Boys. HYBE’s Korean business relies on carefully constructed, mass-appeal acts, and it seems it will try to apply that formula outside South Korea. [...] Lee said Building Weverse will mean more analysis, such as the recent analyses of fan behaviors in the US and Latin markets that HYBE completed.

“These are the kinds of analysis that are needed to understand what types of content needs to be offered, in what sequence we can convert a general music listener [into] a superfan.”

Keeping the cultural differences in mind, HYBE is working on a list of services for non-Korean artists on Weverse so that they can “secure superfans and strengthen their fandom,” Lee said.

Weverse has grown quickly, hitting 10 million monthly active users (MAUs) within a few years of launch, but growth hit a rough patch over the past year. The number of MAUs fell to 9.2 million in Q1 2024, but has since recovered to 9.7 million in Q3.

## Music Business Worldwide

# HYBE wants to change fan culture in US, Latin music, and 4 other things we learned on the company’s latest earnings call

![]()

K-pop giant HYBE has been expanding internationally in recent years, with notable acquisitions in the US and the Latin music market.

But it seems HYBE’s plan isn’t to just acquire labels and leave them on autopilot. On the company’s latest earnings call, execs hinted at an ambitious agenda: changing the nature of fan culture, presumably to make it more like the carefully produced and highly lucrative world of K-pop fandom.

Speaking about the recent launch of **KATSEYE** – the international girl group that HYBE developed with **[Universal Music Group](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/companies/universal-music-group/ "Companies > Universal Music Group [4,581 articles]")** imprint **Geffen Records** – HYBE CEO Jason Jaesang Leesaid the development of KATSEYE is “part of the efforts to revitalize the enthusiastic fandom culture that sort of disappeared in the US after the 1990s.”

That’s certainly an interesting perspective; some would argue that fandom culture didn’t quite disappear, but rather evolved into something new (and decidedly more online).

Yet Lee was likely referring to the popularity of boy and girl groups in the 1990s — think the **Spice Girls** or the **Backstreet Boys**. HYBE’s Korean business relies on carefully constructed, mass-appeal acts, and it seems it will try to apply that formula outside South Korea.

That’s certainly been the case with KATSEYE, which began – like many K-pop acts – with [a broad talent search](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/after-120000-submissions-hybe-and-geffen-unveil-20-artists-to-compete-for-a-spot-in-new-global-girl-group/), followed by an elimination competition that – again like many K-pop acts – was filmed and [turned into a reality show](https://www.netflix.com/hr-en/title/81587828).

“[KATSEYE is] part of the efforts to revitalize the enthusiastic fandom culture that sort of disappeared in the US after the 1990s.”

Jason Jaesang Lee, HYBE

HYBE sees similar potential in the Latin music market as well. Talking about **HYBE Latin America**, the new division formed out of HYBE’s acquisition of **Exile Music**, Lee said HYBE LatAm is “preparing to debut a localized band, which can mark a starting point of [the] fandom business in Latin America, where consumption of Latin genre music can be developed into a more complex culture.”

Translation: Get ready for K-pop-style fandom in the world of Latin music.

Can it work? At the very least, we can say HYBE is being realistic about the effort in that they don’t expect fandoms worldwide to respond to the same things as K-pop’s fandom and are therefore prepared to alter the formula to reflect market realities.

“There are many… differences in terms of characteristics of fans and the kinds of consumption and content that they prefer depending on countries,” Lee said in response to an analyst’s question about the ongoing effort to monetize superfans.

“The number one market is North America, and second is Japan. And for Japan, their characteristics are quite similar to those of K-pop fans. But for the US and Europe, the markets are very different,” Lee added.

He noted that HYBE had “completed an analysis of fan behaviors and their content preferences… for the US and Latin market.”

In the coming months and years, HYBE may give the music industry a strong sense of just how well the K-pop model can be adapted to other regions and musical styles.

HYBE’s earnings call came in the wake of a less-than-stellar [quarterly earnings report](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/hybes-revenue-slides-2-in-q3-amid-olympics-impact-internal-challenges/) from the company, which reported a **2% YoY** decline in revenue in the quarter ended September 30.

Revenue dropped **15.5% YoY** in the “artist direct involvement” segment, which includes recorded music and concerts. That was partly offset by the “artist indirect involvement” segment, which includes the Weverse fan platform, merchandising and licensing, where revenues jumped **31.8% YoY**.

The company’s operating profit fell by over a quarter year over year, and EBITDA shrank by **16.4% YoY**.

HYBE’s execs shared insights on the rapid shift in the company’s revenue mix and other interesting tidbits about its operations and future plans.

Here are four other things we learned on HYBE’s Q3 2024 earnings call…

###### Only 30% of HYBE’s recorded music revenue comes from streaming…

During a breakdown of HYBE’s recorded music revenues, CFO **Kyung-Jun Lee** revealed that “streaming revenue is about one third, or about **30%** of total recorded music sales.”

If that sounds low, it is. Globally, more than two-thirds of recorded music revenue came from streaming, both subscription and ad-supported, according to the [**IFPI**’s report](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/global-recorded-music-revenues-rose-10-2-to-28-6bn-in-2023-as-paid-subscriptions-to-music-streaming-services-exceed-500m-globally12/) for 2023.

At Universal Music Group, the world’s largest music company, streaming revenues accounted for **63.3%** of all [recorded music revenues](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/universal-music-generated-3-15bn-in-q3-subscription-streaming-revenues-grew-8-2-yoy/) in Q3.

HYBE’s low share of streaming revenue is a reflection of the fact that streaming hasn’t had as much uptake in East Asia as it has in other regions.

In Japan, for instance, streaming revenues accounted for just **31.3%** of recorded music revenue in 2023, [according to](https://www.riaj.or.jp/f/e/issue/industry/) the **Recording Industry Association of Japan** (**RIAJ**) – **JYP ¥105.6 billion** out of a total of **¥337.2 billion**.

However, as HYBE internationalizes, we can expect to see a larger share of its revenue come from outside East Asia, and therefore a larger share coming from streaming. In Latin America, one of HYBE’s target regions, streaming accounts for **86.3%** of recorded music revenues, [according to](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/global-recorded-music-revenues-rose-10-2-to-28-6bn-in-2023-as-paid-subscriptions-to-music-streaming-services-exceed-500m-globally12/) the IFPI.

And speaking of HYBE’s internationalization…

###### 2025 will be the year HYBE’s international investments will start paying off..

“2025 will be a year [in which] various businesses that we have been investing for several years will start to produce tangible results,” CFO Kyung-Jun Lee told analysts on the earnings call.

“We expect good earnings results from our diversified music portfolio that has been built through the multi-label strategy, our solutions business – designed to offer new and unique entertainment experiences for our fans – as well as the platform game and AI business.”

That gives us a timeline as to when we can expect to see HYBE’s earnings start to reflect the not insignificant investments the company has been making outside Korea.

There is, of course, HYBE’s [famed 2021 acquisition](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/scooter-braun-sells-ithaca-holdings-to-hybe-formerly-big-hit-entertainment/) of **Scooter Braun**’s **Ithaca Holdings** for more than **$1 billion**, giving the Korean company a solid foothold in the US.

“2025 will be a year [in which] various businesses that we have been investing for several years will start to produce tangible results.”

Kyung-Jun Lee, HYBE

Last year, HYBE expanded into the rapidly growing Latin music market with its [acquisition](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/hybe-acquires-latin-music-label-exile-music-establishes-hybe-latin-america/) of Exile Music, which formed the foundation of the new HYBE Latin America.

Most recently, as part of its new HYBE 2.0 strategy, HYBE [launched a label services business](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/in-case-you-missed-it-hybe-the-company-behind-superstars-bts-and-seventeen-just-launched-a-label-services-business-in-the-us/) in the US and a new division called HYBE MUSIC GROUP APAC, which oversees all of its music label businesses based in Korea and Japan.

All of that investment has been hard to see in HYBE’s earnings this year, as the company continues to feel the drag from its biggest act, **BTS**, being out of the game while its members carry out their mandatory military service.

In other words, those global acquisitions have yet to make HYBE a truly international firm that is not dependent on any one market for its overall health.

That may start to change next year.

###### Declining stock prices and a weak US dollar relative to the won were behind HYBE’s weak net income in Q3…

HYBE’s quarterly net income was one of the most disappointing numbers in the report, disappearing almost entirely in the quarter – down **98.6% YoY** to **KRW 1.4 billion** (**USD $1.03 million** at the average exchange rate for the quarter).

That number was dragged down “due to the recognition of non-operating losses, including valuation losses on equity stakes as well as FX losses on the back of the depreciation of the US dollar,” CFO Lee said on the call.

There certainly was a slide in the value of the US dollar against the Korean won during Q3, making HYBE’s US-dollar earnings less valuable in won, the currency in which HYBE reports.

In fact, from the beginning of Q3 on July 1 to the end of quarter on September 30, the US dollar fell by **5.2%** against the won. But this should prove very temporary: Since the start of October, the dollar has strengthened against the won to its highest levels in around three years. All else being equal, that should make for better numbers in HYBE’s next quarterly report.

The decline in the value of equities owned by HYBE is a less predictable problem. HYBE owns a [little under **10%**](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/hybe-sells-50m-stake-in-sm-entertainment/) of **[SM Entertainment](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/companies/sm-entertainment/ "Companies > SM Entertainment [171 articles]")**, the rival K-pop company over which HYBE lost a bidding war against **Kakao Corp**. last year.

Like other South Korean entertainment companies, SM Entertainment’s share price has been under downward pressure this year, and is down nearly **29%** year on year, as of November 6.

Something similar can be said for HYBE itself: Its stock price is down **6.3%** over the past year. However, markets reacted relatively positively to HYBE’s latest earnings report, and its stock was up **9.6%** since the start of the trading week.

###### HYBE isn’t threatened by potential competition for Weverse

HYBE’s fan platform, **Weverse**, is one of the most closely-watched experiments in monetizing superfans, and it’s one many in the industry hope will succeed – including [recent Weverse investor](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/universal-music-group-strikes-10-year-deal-with-hybe/) **Universal Music Group**.

That said, it’s not the only effort underway to monetize superfans, that segment of the music audience willing to pay more for better access to their favorite acts, exclusive music releases, and merch.

**[Warner Music Group](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/companies/access-industries/warner-music-group/ "Companies > Warner Music Group [3,580 articles]")** is [working on its own superfan app](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/warner-music-group-is-building-a-superfan-app1/); **[Spotify](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/companies/spotify/ "Companies > Spotify [4,737 articles]")** is working on [a new subscription tier](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/daniel-ek-talks-new-spotify-deluxe-tier-price-hikes-the-companys-relationship-with-the-music-industry-and-more-on-q2-earnings-call12/) for superfans; and then there are [all](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/superfan-app-even-launches-with-10000-artists-onboarded-in-app-sales-to-count-towards-billboard-charts/) the [startups](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/fancircles-secures-2m-to-grow-business-building-direct-to-fan-music-streaming-and-superfan-platforms-for-artists/) out [there](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/superfan-platform-mellomanic-formerly-we-are-giant-closes-6m-funding-round/) hoping to become *the* platform for superfans.

So is all this competition a threat to HYBE’s strategy? Not at all, CEO Jaesang Jason Lee says – in fact, it’s a benefit to HYBE’s efforts.

“Right now, Spotify and [Amazon Music](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/companies/amazon/amazon-music/ "Companies > Amazon Music [489 articles]") and other superfan business platforms are growing. And I believe that this is very positive because [it’s] contributing to expanding the superfan business model and market around the world.”

Jason Jaesang Lee, HYBE

“There are a number of tech giants and point players both in Korea and overseas, cooperating and forging partnerships and launching diverse services [for superfans],” Lee said. “And my conclusion is that this type of tendency and trend is favorable for Weverse.”

Lee said that the content and product offerings on Weverse “are not really mature yet,” and the involvement of other major players in establishing a superfan market can help.

“Right now, Spotify and Amazon Music and other superfan business platforms are growing. And I believe that this is very positive because [it’s] contributing to expanding the superfan business model and market around the world.”

Lee said Building Weverse will mean more analysis, such as the recent analyses of fan behaviors in the US and Latin markets that HYBE completed.

“These are the kinds of analysis that are needed to understand what types of content needs to be offered, in what sequence we can convert a general music listener [into] a superfan.”

Keeping the cultural differences in mind, HYBE is working on a list of services for non-Korean artists on Weverse so that they can “secure superfans and strengthen their fandom,” Lee said.

Weverse has grown quickly, hitting **10 million** monthly active users (MAUs) within a few years of launch, but growth hit a rough patch over the past year. The number of MAUs fell to **9.2 million** in Q1 2024, but has since recovered to **9.7 million** in Q3.

![](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/files/2024/11/Weverse-KPI-80x48.png)

Average revenue per paying user (ARPPU) has also declined year over year in the past few years, but HYBE has a plan to fix that: A new higher-priced membership tier that will come with exclusive content and announcements from artists.

[According to news reports](https://www.musicbusinessworldwide.com/hybes-weverse-to-launch-new-superfan-subscription-tiers-and-keep-up-to-60-of-the-money/), some record labels were frustrated with HYBE’s insistence that all labels sign up for the new “digital membership,” from which HYBE will take up to **60%** of revenue.

When asked about this on the earnings call, CEO Lee didn’t deny those news reports, noting only that Weverse users will still have access to many services if they choose not to sign up for the new tier.

HYBE is also upping Weverse’s game in terms of ad-supported revenue. Lee noted that Weverse rolled out in-app ads earlier this year, and plans to expand ad placement to include the app’s home page.

Starting in Q4, video ads will be shown during livestreams and on video-on-demand streams.

“Along with the new monthly subscription membership service to begin in the fourth quarter, the profitability of Weverse is expected to improve in 2025 through new digital business models,” Lee said.Music Business Worldwide
